request.question: What is the name of the regional transportation authority that includes the station which is proximal to Cira Centre South?

title: Tampa Bay Area Regional Transportation Authority
South Florida Regional Transportation Authority
Santa Ana Regional Transportation Center
30th Street Station
Berks Area Regional Transportation Authority
Anaheim Regional Transportation Intermodal Center
Franklin Street station (Pennsylvania)
William F. Walsh Regional Transportation Center
Tri-Rail
Cira Centre South
sentences: The Tampa Bay Area Regional Transportation Authority, or TBARTA, is a regional transportation agency of the U.S. state of Florida which was created on July 1, 2007.

The purpose of the agency is "to plan, develop, finance, construct, own, purchase, operate, maintain, relocate, equip, repair, and manage multimodal systems in Citrus, Hernando, Hillsborough, Manatee, Pasco, Pinellas, and Sarasota Counties."
The agency coordinates its efforts with the Florida Department of Transportation to improve transportation in the Tampa Bay Area.
The South Florida Regional Transportation Authority (SFRTA) is a tri-county public transit authority operating out of Pompano Beach, Florida, serving Miami-Dade, Broward and Palm Beach counties.
The organization was created on July 1, 2003 by the Florida Legislature and enacted by the Florida Department of Transportation.

Replacing the pre-existing Tri-County Commuter Rail Authority, the goal of incorporation was to expand cooperation between the Tri-Rail commuter rail service and the existing county public transport authorities: Broward County Transit, Miami-Dade Transit, and Palm Tran.
Tri-Rail is solely operated by SFRTA.
Santa Ana Regional Transportation Center is a passenger rail station and transportation center in Santa Ana, California.
It is used by Amtrak's "Pacific Surfliner" and Metrolink's Orange County Line and Inland Empire-Orange County Line trains.
It is also a Greyhound station and a hub for the Orange County Transportation Authority bus system as well as a terminal for several Mexican bus tour companies.

The 30th Street Station is the main railroad station in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, United States and one of the seven stations in Southeastern Pennsylvania Transportation Authority's (SEPTA) Center City fare zone.
It is also a major stop on Amtrak's (National Railroad Passenger Corporation) Northeast and Keystone Corridors.

Berks Area Regional Transportation Authority (BARTA), previously Berks Area Reading Transportation Authority, is a public transportation system serving the city of Reading and its surrounding area of Berks County, Pennsylvania.
Dennis D. Louwerse, the long-time executive director/CEO at BARTA, died on Thursday, September 5, 2013 at the age of 68.
He became the executive director at BARTA in 1983, and he was the executive director for 30 years until his death in 2013.
David W. Kilmer is currently the executive director at BARTA and Red Rose Transit Authority.
The Anaheim Regional Transportation Intermodal Center (ARTIC) is an intermodal transit center in Anaheim, California, United States.

It serves as a train station for Amtrak intercity rail and Metrolink commuter rail, as well as a bus station used by the Orange County Transportation Authority (OCTA), Anaheim Resort Transportation (ART), Greyhound, Megabus, and Tres Estrellas de Oro.
Franklin Street station is a bus station and former railroad station in Reading, Pennsylvania.
It currently serves Berks Area Regional Transportation Authority (BARTA) buses and hosts a transportation museum.
The William F. Walsh Regional Transportation Center is the long-distance ground travel (rail and bus) terminal serving the Syracuse, New York area.
It is served by Amtrak, Greyhound Lines, Megabus, and Trailways.
Local and regional bus transportation is provided by the Central New York Regional Transportation Authority (CENTRO).
Various taxi firms service the Center, as well.
The Regional Transportation Center is located on the north side of Syracuse, adjacent to the Central New York Regional Market and near Destiny USA.
Tri-Rail (reporting mark TRCX) is a commuter rail line linking Miami, Fort Lauderdale, and West Palm Beach, Florida, United States.
It is managed by the South Florida Regional Transportation Authority (SFRTA) along CSX Transportation's former Miami Subdivision, the line now wholly owned by the Florida DOT.

The 70.9 mi system has 18 stations along the Southeast Florida coast, and connects directly to Amtrak at numerous stations, and to Metrorail at the Tri-Rail and Metrorail Transfer Station and at Miami Airport station.
Cira Centre South is a complex of two skyscrapers in the University City district of Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, which is across the Schuylkill River from Center City Philadelphia.
The location of the buildings is between Walnut Street and Chestnut Street south of 30th Street Station and the Old Post Office Building.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: What is the name of the regional transportation authority that includes the station which is proximal to Cira Centre South?
hop1_docs: Cira Centre South | Cira Centre South is a complex of two skyscrapers in the University City district of Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, which is across the Schuylkill River from Center City Philadelphia.  The location of the buildings is between Walnut Street and Chestnut Street south of 30th Street Station and the Old Post Office Building.

Cira Centre | The Cira Centre is a 29-story, 437 ft office high-rise in the University City section of Philadelphia, across the street from Amtrak's 30th Street Station.  Developed by Brandywine Realty Trust and designed by César Pelli, it was built in 2004-05 on a platform over rail tracks.

Transportation in Boston | The Boston transportation system includes roadway, subway, regional rail, air, and sea options for passenger and freight transit in Boston, Massachusetts.  The Massachusetts Port Authority (Massport) operates the Port of Boston, which includes a container shipping facility in South Boston, and Logan International Airport, in East Boston.  The Massachusetts Bay Transportation Authority (MBTA) operates bus, subway, short distance rail, and water ferry passenger services throughout the city and region.  Amtrak operates passenger rail service to and from major northeastern cities.  A major bus terminal at South Station is served by varied intercity bus companies.  The city is bisected by major highways I-90 and I-93, the intersection of which has undergone a major renovation, nicknamed the Big Dig.

Canadian Internet Registration Authority | The Canadian Internet Registration Authority (CIRA) (French: Autorité canadienne pour les enregistrements Internet ACEI) is the organization that manages the . CA country code top-level domain, the policies that support Canada’s Internet community and Canada’s involvement in international Internet governance.  CIRA is a member-driven organization.  Membership in CIRA is included in the cost of a . CA domain, and open to anyone who holds a . CA domain.  There are approximately 15,000 CIRA members as of January 2013.

Metrolinx | Metrolinx is a Crown agency that manages and integrates road and public transport in the Golden Horseshoe region, which includes the cities of Toronto and Hamilton, in the province of Ontario in Canada.  Headquartered in Union Station in Toronto, the organization was created by the Government of Ontario as the "Greater Toronto Transportation Authority" on April 24, 2006.  It adopted the public name, Metrolinx, in 2007.

Transit-proximate development | Transit-proximate development is a term used by some planning officials to describe (potentially dense) development that is physically near a public transport node (e.g. a bus station, train station or metro station).  This type of development includes transit-oriented development, but, according to some planning officials, can also describe development that is not transit-oriented development.  Thus, transit-proximate development can include results where, despite the location of dense development near transit, the development does not take full advantage of -- or fully encourage the use of -- the public transport node.  For example, transit-proximate development could include buildings with extensive parking facilities typical of suburban locations, a lack of "mixed-use development" (housing, workplaces and shopping in the same place), or a lack of extensive pedestrian facilities that would make it easier for people to reach the public transport node.

Glenanda | Glenanda is a suburb of Johannesburg, South Africa.  It is located in Region F.  Glenanda borders the suburbs of Glenvista, Suideroord and Winchester Hills.  Glenanda has its own public library, service station and local shopping centre.  A primary school is established in Glenanda which services the surrounding areas.  Glenanda is in close proximity to The Glen Shopping Centre with an average travelling time of five minutes.
response.summary_1:
- Fact: [1] — Cira Centre South is "south of 30th Street Station" (quoted: "south of 30th Street Station").
- Fact: [2] — Cira Centre (related complex) is "across the street from Amtrak's 30th Street Station" (quoted: "across the street from Amtrak's 30th Street Station").
- Bridge entity (explicitly present): 30th Street Station (mentioned in [1] and [2]).
- Missing/ambiguous fact: The passages do not state which regional transportation authority "includes" or operates/serves 30th Street Station (e.g., SEPTA vs. Amtrak or other agencies) — this fact is required to answer the question.
- Next-hop retrieval targets (prioritized):
  1) Fetch the "30th Street Station" page (Wikipedia article titled "30th Street Station" or "Philadelphia 30th Street Station"); extract the infobox and lead lines for "Owned by", "Operated by", "Services" or "Served by" (look for listings of Amtrak, SEPTA Regional Rail, and any regional authority names).
  2) Fetch the "Southeastern Pennsylvania Transportation Authority" (SEPTA) page; extract station lists or "Regional Rail" service maps/stop lists and search the page for "30th Street Station" to confirm inclusion.
  3) If needed, fetch the "Amtrak" and Amtrak station directory pages to confirm Amtrak's role vs. a regional authority.
- No final answer given: required operator/authority for 30th Street Station is not present or unambiguous in the supplied passages.
response.hop2_query: 30th Street Station operator regional transportation authority
hop2_docs: 30th Street Station | The 30th Street Station is the main railroad station in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, United States and one of the seven stations in Southeastern Pennsylvania Transportation Authority's (SEPTA) Center City fare zone.  It is also a major stop on Amtrak's (National Railroad Passenger Corporation) Northeast and Keystone Corridors.

30th & Downing station | 30th & Downing station is a RTD light rail station in the Five Points neighborhood of Denver, Colorado, United States.  Operating as part of the D Line, the station was opened on October 8, 1994, and is operated by the Regional Transportation District.  It is the current northern terminus for Five Points trains.  Currently there is only one track on Welton Street for light rail trains, necessitating track sharing for trains in both directions between here and 20th & Welton.  Therefore, only one line serves this station and all stations on the Five Points branch.

Franklin Street station (Pennsylvania) | Franklin Street station is a bus station and former railroad station in Reading, Pennsylvania.  It currently serves Berks Area Regional Transportation Authority (BARTA) buses and hosts a transportation museum.

Orchard (VTA) | Orchard is a light rail station operated by Santa Clara Valley Transportation Authority.  The station is in San Jose, California, in the center median of 1st Street near Orchard Parkway.  The station's street address is 3060 N. First Street.

Component (VTA) | Component is a light rail station operated by Santa Clara Valley Transportation Authority.  The station is located in San Jose, California in the center median of 1st Street near Component Drive.  The station's street address is 2540 N. First Street.

Karina (VTA) | Karina is a light rail station operated by Santa Clara Valley Transportation Authority.  The station is located in San Jose, California in the center median of 1st Street near Karina Court.  The station's street address is 1900 N. First Street.

West Side Yard | The West Side Yard (officially the John D. Caemmerer West Side Yard) is a rail yard owned by the Metropolitan Transportation Authority on the west side of Manhattan in New York City.  Used to store commuter rail trains operated by the Long Island Rail Road, the 26.17 acres yard sits between West 30th Street, West 33rd Street, 10th Avenue and 12th Avenue.
response.summary_2: The regional transportation authority is the Southeastern Pennsylvania Transportation Authority (SEPTA). 30th Street Station is the main railroad station in Philadelphia and a major stop on Amtrak's Northeast and Keystone Corridors.  Southeastern Pennsylvania Transportation Authority includes 30th Street Station as one of the seven stations in its Center City fare zone.
